coredata.dc:title: Dual-recognition immune-co-chemical ECL-sensor based on Ti,Mg@N-CDs-induced and novel signal-sensing units Poly(DVB-co-PBA)-reported for alpha-fetoprotein detection
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Jianping
Li, Shijie
Wang, Junying
Wang, Junping
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a high-efficiency electrochemiluminescence (ECL) ternary immune-system was firstly constructed for ultrasensitive assay of alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) based on the Ti, N/Mg co-doped carbon dots (Ti,Mg@N-CDs) as brand-new nanocomposite emitter, Poly(DVB-Co-PBA) as novel AFP-signal reporter and coreaction accelerator. A fluorescent microspheres Poly(DVB-Co-PBA), synthesizing based on 4-vinylphenylboronic acid (4-MPBA) and divinylbenzene (DVB), was used as a signal indicator-probe via the specific carbohydrate-AFP interaction to fabricate a dual-recognition label-free immune-co-chemical biosensor. The as-prepared Ti,Mg@N-CDs exhibited high ECL emission of 3.7-folds compared with CDs. Moreover, the large surface area, promising biocompatibility and nitrogen-rich functional group bearing on the nanohybrid was helpful for the antibody adsorption immobilization of AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE. The successful synthesis and enhancement mechanism of the nanomaterials were also characterized through a serious of techniques including HR-TEM, SEM, EDS, FT-IR, UV–vis, PL and CV. With an immune-co-chemical ECL-assay format, target and signal reporter-probe (Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)) were introduced in sequence via specific reaction. Based on multiple signal amplification and dual recognition strategy, a largely linear detection ranges from 2.25 × 10−6 to 2250 ng mL-1 with a low detection limit (1.55 × 10-7 ng mL-1) were obtained via the resultant ECL ternary (Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)-AFP/AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE) system. This proposed new method was applied to detect human serum samples with satisfactory results and acceptable recovery (96.04 % ∼ 111.22 %), indicating a potential application in clinical monitoring of disease biomarkers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alpha-fetoprotein
Aminated nano-titanium dioxide
Ti, N/Mg co-doped carbon dots
Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)
Immune-co-chemical ECL-assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical performance and possible ECL mechanism of Ti,Mg@N-CDs
Optimization of synthesis conditions of nanocomposites and test conditions
The detection of AFP in human serum sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Tianjin University of Science and Technology

National Key R&D Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Innovation Project of Excellent Doctorial Dissertation of Tianjin University of Science and Technology (No. 2020010 ), The 68th China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2020M680870 ), “ The National Key R&D Program of China ” (No. 2016YFD0401202 ).

This research was supported by Innovation Project of Excellent Doctorial Dissertation of Tianjin University of Science and Technology (No. 2020010), The 68th China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M680870), ?The National Key R&D Program of China? (No. 2016YFD0401202).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis and light-emitting mechanism of Ti-doped Mg@N-CDs (A), (B) and the design and fabrication of the immune-co-chemical ECL-sensor (C).
HR-TEM images of the prepared NH2-TiO2 (A); Mg@N-CDs (B); Ti-doped Mg@N-CDs (C, D).

(A) EDS spectra of Ti-doped Mg@N-CDs; (B) ECL intensity of Mg@N-CDs/GCE and Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE stored in the refrigerator for 10 days (detected every two day); (C)/(D) FL emission spectra of Ti-doped Mg@N-CDs under excitation at different wavelengths.
Characterization of prepared Ti-doped Mg@N-CDs. (A) FT–IR spectra; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra; (C) Fluorescence spectra (FL, Ex:360 nm) and (D) ECL spectra.

(A) ECL curves of bare GCE (a), NH2-TiO2/GCE (b), Mg@N-CDs/GCE (c) and Ti-doped Mg@N-CDs/GCE (d) in 100 mM K2S2O8 solution (PBS, 0.1 M, pH 7.4); (B) EIS of GCE (a), NH2-TiO2/GCE (b), Mg@N-CDs/GCE (c) and Ti-doped Mg@N-CDs/GCE (d) in 0.2 M KNO3 containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−; (C) Cyclic voltammogram curve of NH2-TiO2/GCE, Mg@N-CDs/GCE and Ti-doped Mg@N-CDs/GCE; (D) ECL intensity-potential curve of immune-co-chemical sensor with AFP, Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)-reporter and without AFP.
Characterization of prepared AFP-reporter (Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)). (A) SEM images of the prepared Poly(DVB-Co-PBA); (B) FT–IR spectra; (C) Size distribution; (D) Fluorescence spectra (FL, Ex:258 nm).

The optimizations of (A) the ultrasonication time of Ti-doped Mg@N-CDs; (B) Ratio of Mg@N-CDs to NH2-TiO2; (C) different concentrations of AFP antibody caused time-dependent ECL-changes of Ti-doped Mg@N-CDs/GCE; (D), (E) incubation time/temperature of the AFP on AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE; (F) pH value of the Poly(DVB-Co-PBA) (AFP-reporter) solution. Each set of data was three repeated measurements.

(A) ECL intensity-time curves of the prepared immune-co-chemical ECL-sensor incubated with various concentration of AFP (up to down: 2.25 × 10−6 ∼ 2250 ng mL-1); (B) Standard curve of immune-co-chemical ECL-sensor (S/N = 3).

(A) Selectivity assessment for AFP detection against other interference: CEA, PSA, TRF, BSA, lgG, glucose and the mixture with AFP. (B) Stability test of the proposed ECL-sensor in eleven consecutive tests. (C) Reproducibility of the designed ECL-sensors in seven parallel experiments with AFP concentration of 2.25 × 10−3 ng mL-1. (D) Regenerativity of the designed ECL-sensor.
Comparison of different methods for the determination of AFP.
Recovery experiments for sensing AFP in the spiked serum samples from human.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Recovery (%) = Found concentration/(origin concentration + spiked concentration) × 100 %.

head.ce:title: Dual-recognition immune-co-chemical ECL-sensor based on Ti,Mg@N-CDs-induced and novel signal-sensing units Poly(DVB-co-PBA)-reported for alpha-fetoprotein detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science & Technology, 29 The Thirteenth Road, Tianjin Economy and Technology Development Area, Tianjin, 300457, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Tianjin Economy and Technology Development Area

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science & Technology, 29 The Thirteenth Road, Tianjin Economy and Technology Development Area, Tianjin, 300457, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Biotechnology Research Institute (BRI) of Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences (CAAS), PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Biotechnology Research Institute (BRI) of Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences (CAAS)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Biotechnology Research Institute (BRI) of Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences (CAAS)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a high-efficiency electrochemiluminescence (ECL) ternary immune-system was firstly constructed for ultrasensitive assay of alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) based on the Ti, N/Mg co-doped carbon dots (Ti,Mg@N-CDs) as brand-new nanocomposite emitter, Poly(DVB-Co-PBA) as novel AFP-signal reporter and coreaction accelerator. A fluorescent microspheres Poly(DVB-Co-PBA), synthesizing based on 4-vinylphenylboronic acid (4-MPBA) and divinylbenzene (DVB), was used as a signal indicator-probe via the specific carbohydrate-AFP interaction to fabricate a dual-recognition label-free immune-co-chemical biosensor. The as-prepared Ti,Mg@N-CDs exhibited high ECL emission of 3.7-folds compared with CDs. Moreover, the large surface area, promising biocompatibility and nitrogen-rich functional group bearing on the nanohybrid was helpful for the antibody adsorption immobilization of AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE. The successful synthesis and enhancement mechanism of the nanomaterials were also characterized through a serious of techniques including HR-TEM, SEM, EDS, FT-IR, UV–vis, PL and CV. With an immune-co-chemical ECL-assay format, target and signal reporter-probe (Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)) were introduced in sequence via specific reaction. Based on multiple signal amplification and dual recognition strategy, a largely linear detection ranges from 2.25 × 10−6 to 2250 ng mL-1 with a low detection limit (1.55 × 10-7 ng mL-1) were obtained via the resultant ECL ternary (Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)-AFP/AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE) system. This proposed new method was applied to detect human serum samples with satisfactory results and acceptable recovery (96.04 % ∼ 111.22 %), indicating a potential application in clinical monitoring of disease biomarkers.

ce:section.ce:para: The risk of hepatic carcinoma (HCC) is especially prevalent for malignant tumors occurred around the world [1,2], in China, which are accounted for half of the world’s total new HCC cases and deaths [3]. However, the HCC in the early-stage is hardly diagnosed since there is no special symptoms, which often delays the patients' optimal treatment-time [4]. Thus, early ultra-sensitive and rapid monitoring of the abnormal content change of disease biomarker in biological fluids is becoming significant to the early disease diagnosis. As a tumor marker, α-fetoprotein (AFP) is an oncofetal glycoprotein with a molecular mass of approximately 70 kDa, which is mainly produced by the liver, yolk sac, and gastrointestinal tract of a human fetus [5]. However, it may also be found at high levels (more than 25 ng/mL) in the sera of adults having certain malignancies. The increased AFP concentration in adult plasma is usually considered as an early indication of hepatocellular carcinoma or endodermal sinus tumor [6]. Currently, Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) has been widely utilized as an important biomarker for fetal defect and hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). Tremendous research enthusiasm has been devoted to develop reliable strategies for the detection of AFP.

Recently, immunoassay combined with fluorescence [7], chemiluminescence assay [8], quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) [9], colorimetric and electrochemistry elegant methods [10], etc. have been reported for tests of AFP. Unfortunately, the methods above are usually single-mode signal readout. To address these issues, various dual-signal readout strategies have been proposed for the detection of the disease biomarkers. Visualization with naked eye combined with other quantitative analysis strategies have been sufficiently explored as the most promising approaches. The uncertainty, low sensitivity or even the appearance of “false positive” test results of the visual analysis strategy is non-negligible due to different operators and non-standard test procedures. Based on the above-mentioned views, an accurate, rapid, and sensitive analytical method for AFP determination is needed.

Boronate-affinity chromatography (BACs) [11] is a chemical analysis-method based on the covalent interaction between boron-affinity materials and the cis-o-dihydroxy group in the target. The BACs has many advantages: (a) The force of boron-affinity is stronger (binding constant 103 M−1) than the non-covalent bond; (b) It is generally analyzed in acidic-system and has good MS-compatibility; (c) Capturing and releasing the target molecule through esterification reaction, which protect the structure of the analytes; (d) Control the adsorption only by adjusting the pH of solution without extra conditions. Given their superior properties, BACs has been demonstrated the promising applications in different fields of enrichment, separation [12], imaging and sensing [13]. Only few reports, however, have focused on exploring the integrated-technology of BACs-method and ECL immunity-strategy for AFP detection in clinical medicine.

As an emerging developed analytical method for the detection of biomolecules, electrochemiluminescence immunity-strategy [14,15] possesses the advantages of high selectivity, low consumption and simplicity. Immunity-ECL probes are increasingly used for biomarkers detection and provide remarkable results in early-detection [16], which is a crucial element for the timely diagnosis and successful treatment of all human cancers. As well known, ECL-signal amplification of immunity-ECL relied extensively on the superior nano-luminophores for increasing detection sensitivity. In past studies, a variety of ECL luminophores were mainly focused on luminol [17], bipyridylium [18], SnS2 quantum dots (SnS2-QDs) and upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) [19]. Despite the strong ECL-signal, the high toxicity/cost and complicated preparation process limit their wide applications. Therefore, the key task in immunity-ECL research has been to explore the innovative, environmental-friendly, steady and high-efficiency ECL luminophores.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new biocompatible and light-responsive nanomaterial, have been found extensive applications in biomarkers, photocatalysis, sensing and biomedicine ﬁelds due to optical-stability, easy-functionalization and low-toxicity [20,21]. However, poor ECL-signal and unclear luminescence mechanism severely hamper its wide application in ECL-sensors. It reported that previous studies have improved ECL-intensity of CDs by introducing co-reactive reagents. Li et al. [22] has synthesized TiO2@SnS2 green luminophore with excellent ECL-signal successfully based on synergic combination between SnS2 and TiO2. A novel material-synthesis strategy to improve the ECL-signal of CDs was provided for us. Semiconductor material TiO2 not only has film-forming, chemical and thermal stability and the harmless to the human body, but also has good photocatalytic performance due to its special band structure [23]. In particular, TiO2-based composites have proved to be an excellent electrode material in photoelectrochemical biosensing owing to their good biocompatibility, relative conductivity and inexpensiveness. Recently, some applications of titanium dioxide in electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensor have been reported, such as TiO2-CNTs [24], CeO2@TiO2 [25], ZnO/TiO2 [26] and TiO2-CdSe [27]. Thus, NH2-TiO2 was applied to the supporting platform and as a signal amplifier of the CDs in this work.

Inspired by above explorations, we synthesized a novel environmental-friendly nano-luminophore Ti, N/Mg co-doped carbon dots (Ti,Mg@N-CDs) by introducing photocatalyst/NH2-TiO2 using chemical ultrasonic-method. An outstanding ECL-signal of 3.7-folds than Mg@N-CDs was obtained at -2.1 V. The amino group in NH2-TiO2 on the Ti,Mg@N-CDs rendered the nanoparticles water-soluble and also enabled conjugation of the NH2-TiO2 with Mg@N-CDs through hydrogen bonding. Compared to traditional precipitation-method, ultrasonic-method simplifies its complicated synthetic procedures. Moreover, the size and morphology of Ti,Mg@N-CDs are easy to control utilizing the dispersion and shear-breaking of ultrasound. On this basis, fluorescence poly(DVB-co-PBA) microsphere based on 4-vinylphenylboronic acid (4-MPBA) and divinylbenzene (DVB) was prepared through free radical polymerization-way and can be applicated in glycoproteins recognition. The 4-MPBA of poly(DVB-co-PBA) could capture AFP through esterification of boronic acid groups with cis-o-dihydroxy groups in glycoprotein AFP. Meanwhile, the five-membered cyclic borate structure of AFP-Poly(DVB-Co-PBA) bioconjugate, as an electron donor, were used to enhance ECL intensity with the increase of the AFP. However, the integrated-technology of BACs-method and ECL immunity-strategy for AFP detection is scarce.

Herein, we present a high-efficiency immune-co-chemical ECL ternary sensing platform (Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)-AFP/AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE) to achieve highly sensitive and specific assay of AFP. As expressed in Scheme 1 A, Ti,Mg@N-CDs, combination of N@Mg-CDs as luminophore and NH2-TiO2 as coreaction accelerator, was synthesized and modified on the GCE surface, obtaining a strong ECL signal (ECL-trigger). AntiAFP was heavily attached on Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE owing to the good biocompatibility and nitrogen-rich functional group [28]. Subsequently, ECL signal was quenched (“signal off” state). In the presence of AFP, Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)-AFP bioconjugates are formed on AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE, in which Poly(DVB-Co-PBA) as a dual recognizer and ECL-resuming signal-reporter (“signal on” state). Meanwhile, Poly(DVB-co-PBA) overcomes the challenge that organic molecules need to be further loaded on the surface of SiO2 NPs or polymer spheres to gather their signals [29]. Geting its ECL-signals through simple chemical reaction and used as a signal-reporter to detect AFP sensitively. Finally, such proposed Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)-AFP/AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE achieved highly sensitive, accurate and specific analysis of AFP with an immune-co-chemical format and multiple signal amplification and dual recognition strategy. Significantly, this work offers a ternary-immune biosensing platform for other tumor biomarkers assay by slightly changing the corresponding antibody.
All chemicals and related instruments used in the experiments have been listed in supporting information.

The N/Mg co-doped carbon dots (Mg@N-CDs) was synthesized according to previous reports with a slightly adjustment [30]. Synthesis of Mg@N-CDs by one-step hydrothermal method: 4.2 g of magnesium hydroxide and 5 mL of ethylenediamine were slowly dissolved into 10 g-40 mL citric-acid/de) ionized water under continuous stirring. Subsequently, the resultant solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and then heated to 200 °C for 3 h. After being cooled down to room temperature naturally, the brownish-yellow precipitates were collected by centrifugation and rinsed several times with deionized water and ethanol. Finally, the obtained powder (Mg@N-CDs) was freeze-dried overnight and protected from light at 4 °C. A novel nano-composite luminophores Ti,Mg@N-CDs was synthetized with a volume ratio of Mg@N-CDs to NH2-TiO2 of 1:1.5 using chemical ultrasonic-method by hydrogen bonding OH⋯N.
In this study, Poly(DVB-Co-PBA) was prepared and full details can be found in the supplementary material.

First of all, working electrodes (GCE, 4 mm) needed to be polished with alumina slurry (1.0, 0.05 μm), which ensuring the peak-to-peak potential measured via CV in Fe(CN)6 3− solution containing 0.2 M KCl was below 80 mV. Then, 10 μL Ti,Mg@N-CDs solution was coated on the surface of bare GCE by physical adsorption. For the immune-co-chemical ECL-sensor fabricated was as follows (Scheme 1B, C): First, the exposed carboxyl groups on Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE were activated by 0.4 M EDC and 0.1 mM NHS for 2 h at room temperature. Then, 15 μL of diluted antibody solution (50 μg mL-1) was dropped onto the surface of nitrogen-riched/activated Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE for 3 h at 4 °C for immobilization. The unbound antibodies were rinsed completely by PBST (0.05 %, pH 7.4), following non-specific active sites on the ECL-trigger layer were blocked by 1 % BSA for 30 min at room temperature. Subsequently, AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE were immersed into AFP-incubation buffer containing different concentrations of AFP for binding the sites of the AntiAFP for 25 min at 37 °C. The ECL intensity of AFP/AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE (F0, a.u.) was recorded. Finally, AFP/AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE was immersed into Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)-buﬀ ;er (pH, 9.0) solution and then reacted for 30 min. The resulting intensity (F, a.u.) of Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)-AFP/AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE was recorded until ECL-signal became steady.

The process of ECL-resuming was performed by shaking the AFP-AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE electrodes (F0 , a.u.) in Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)-solution (1 mg/mL) for 20 min with magnetically stirring. In a typical procedure, the target (AFP) and signal reporter-probe (Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)) were anchored based on the covalent interaction between Poly(DVB-Co-PBA) and the cis-o-dihydroxy group in glycoprotein AFP. The resuming-ECL intensities of Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)-AFP/AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE (F, a.u.) were recorded until ECL-signal became steady. Finally, the changes of intensity (F-F0 , a.u.) was calculated.

All work-electrodes adsorbed were detected in 1 mL of PBS-K2S2O8 buffer (0.1 mol·L−1 K2S2O8, PBS: 0.1 mol L−1, pH 7.4). All ECL data was acquired via cyclic voltammetry and electrochemiluminescence simultaneously. The detection conditions were that the cathode potential range was 0 V ∼ −2.1 V, the scanning speed was 100 mV·s−1, and the photomultiplier tube (PMT) was set at 650 V.

Normal human serum was filtered using a 3 kDa dialysis bag to remove possible interfered compounds before use, while AFP passed through. Then, the sera were diluted appropriately with 10 mM phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7.4). For the real sample, the different concentrations of AFP (0.01, 0.10, 1.00, 3.00, 5.00 and 10.00 ng mL−1) were spiked into for assessing the applicability of the developed immune-co-chemical ECL-sensor.

The morphologies, microstructures and spectra of the prepared Ti,Mg@N-CDs and Mg@N-CDs were characterized and compared in details. HR-TEM was used to demonstrated the detailed microstructures of the obtained nano luminophore-Ti,Mg@N-CDs. The image of Fig. 1 A showed a clear lattice fringe of 0.31 nm and could be assigned to the (011) plane of nano NH2-TiO2. As shown in Fig. 1B, Mg@N-CDs exhibited almost monodisperse particles with an average diameter of 10.2 ± 0.3 nm. By compared, the smaller average size of Ti,Mg@N-CDs nanoparticle was obtained at 3.0 nm (Fig. 1C). Moreover, HR-TEM image (Fig. 1D) showed lattice fringes of 0.33 nm and could be assigned to the (021) plane of Ti,Mg@N-CDs [31]. The HR-TEM images shed light, to some extent, on the mechanism of formation for the Ti,Mg@N-CDs. In the synthesis process, the chemical-ultrasonic treatment led to the change of the particle size. And photocatalytic NH2-TiO2 improved the ECL-performance of the Ti,Mg@N-CDs [32]. The Ti,Mg@N-CDs as the substrate provided larger surface areas to load on GCE, which were beneficial for facilely capturing the AntiAFP.

Energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) spectra were employed to reveal the presence of different components in Ti,Mg@N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, C, N and O atoms occupy most of element mass. Small amounts of Mg atoms coexist with dominant Ti atoms. N and O is derived from the NH2-TiO2 and Mg@N-CDs. Ti and Mg, C only come from the NH2-TiO2, Mg@N-CDs, respectively. The EDS analysis results further reveal the synthesis of Ti,Mg@N-CDs successfully. ECL stabilities were studied by measuring and comparing ECL intensity (Fig. 2B). With the extending of storage time from 0 to 10 d, the ECL intensity of Mg@N-CDs/GCE dramatically decreases to 206 a.u. (initial value is 3897 a.u.), while that of Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE has negligible changes (more than 0.93). These experimental results suggest that the film-forming/photocatalytic NH2-TiO2 made considerable progress in ECL efficiency and stability of Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE [33].

Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra testify that facile combination of NH2-TiO2 nanoparticles with Mg@N-CDs cannot distinctly influence on their characteristic vibration bands (Fig. 3 A). Only the bands at 725 cm−1 is the broad and intense peak of Ti-O (Ti,Mg@N-CDs(c)). Thus, it proved that the successful synthesis of Ti,Mg@N-CDs is attributed to the intermolecular force (OH⋯N-bond) between Mg@N-CDs and NH2-TiO2. UV–vis absorption spectrum is also carried out to confirm the formation of Ti,Mg@N-CDs. In the UV–vis spectrum of Mg@N-CDs solution (Fig. 3B(b)), a strong band at 241 nm and 352 nm were observed, which can be contributed to the π-π* transition of CC bonds and n-π* transition of CO bonds, respectively. NH2-TiO2 had a characteristic absorption peak at 234 nm (Fig. 3B(c)). Curve c show that the UV–vis absorption obviously insufficient after 350 nm, which is derived from wide band gap of TiO2 [34]. Another absorbance bands at 242 nm appeared in the spectrum of Ti,Mg@N-CDs (curve a) as accompanied by the disappearances of the ultraviolet peak of NH2-TiO2 and Mg@N-CDs. Meanwhile, it obviously shows the enhanced absorption from 200 nm to 400 nm, which may ascribe to the formation of transfer-state of charge between defected-state of NH2-TiO2 and Mg@N-CDs. FL spectra further reveal the improved FL intensity of Ti,Mg@N-CDs compared Mg@N-CDs. The ECL spectra of Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE was showed in Fig. 3D. The ECL spectrum of Ti,Mg@N-CDs achieving maximum at 520 nm, which presented an obvious red shift comparing PL intensity at 445 nm. It may be caused by two different light-emitting mechanisms. To our best knowledge, a blue emission at 445 nm attributinmission spectrum of Mg@N-CDs is mainly caused by the transition of electrons. For ECL process, the possible mechanism may be interpreted by electron transfer, what is illustrated later in the article [22]. Ti,Mg@N-CDs present the excitation-independent FL emission (445 nm) (Fig. 2C, D). As illustrated in Figure, with increasing the excitation of Ti,Mg@N-CDs in the range of 300−360 nm (Fig. 2C), the fluorescence intensity at 445 nm increased gradually and then weaked until disappeared when the excitation wavelength at 370−420 nm (Fig. 2D). The superior excitation-independent FL feature of Ti,Mg@N-CDs is mainly related to the oxidation-degree of the surface. The lower-oxidation degree and fewer surface-defects of Ti,Mg@N-CDs based on TiO2 coordinated with Mg@N-CDs via OH⋯N bonding may cause the excitation-independent FL emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance and possible ECL mechanism of Ti,Mg@N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the electrochemical performance of the proposed immune-co-chemical ECL-sensor in different steps, GCE electrode was assembled and characterized by ECL and EIS. ECL proﬁles in Fig. 4 A showed the characterization of bare GCE (a), NH2-TiO2/GCE (b), Mg@N-CDs/GCE (c) and Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE (d). Firstly, the bare GCE electrode (curve a) exhibited no obvious ECL signal (∼204 a.u.). When NH2-TiO2-film and Mg@N-CDs was modiﬁed on bare GCE, respectively, the ECL intensity ∼758 a.u. and ∼3204 a.u. were obtained. As shown in curve (d), the ECL intensity of Ti,Mg@N-CDs improved signiﬁcantly presenting the highest ECL emission (∼11,704 a.u.), which was 3.7-fold that of Mg@N-CDs. The result implied that TiO2 can indeed have signal amplification effect on Ti,Mg@N-CDs. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) characterization results after the layer-by-layer modification were showed in Fig. 4B. We can estimate the speed of electron transfer by electron-transfer resistance (Ret). Ret value of the unmodified bare GCE was measured to be 230 Ω (curve a), while that of the NH2-TiO2/GCE and Mg@N-CDs/GCE were 370 Ω and 705 Ω, respectively (curve b, c), suggesting that NH2 and COOH formed an additional barrier. Noticeably, the Ret of AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE (1991 Ω, curve e) was much larger than Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE, which was ascribed to the insulating property of antibody molecules and the steric hindrance effect. The ECL results together with EIS results further support the successful establishment of the ECL-sensor as expected.

To explain the enhancement of the nano NH2-TiO2 to ECL signals, cyclic voltammograms (CVs) was measured during a potential scan cycle −2.5 ∼ 0 V. As shown in Fig. 4C, the corresponding CV curve of Mg@N-CDs exhibited two evident reduction peaks at −1.1 V and −1.62 V in PBS-K2S2O8 system. It is well known that S2O8 2− can be electrochemically reduced to a strong oxidation intermediate (SO4 ·-). And the corresponding reduction peak in this system was at −1.1 V. The peak at −1.62 V indicate the negatedively charged Mg@N-CDs (Mg@N-CDs·-) were produced by injecting electrons into the unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) [35]. In comparison to the electroreduction peak at −1.62 V of Mg@N-CDs, Ti,Mg@N-CDs exhibited more positive reduction potentials (−1.45 V) due to the small energy gap of NH2-TiO2. More, NH2-TiO2 with more positive reduction potentials (−1.2 V) could speed up the reduction of S2O8 2− to form the excited state of Ti,Mg@N-CDs* for releasing stronger ECL signals. These results indicated that the better ECL-signal of the Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE was the result of the interaction between the Mg@N-CDs and the NH2-TiO2. The ECL reaction mechanism of Ti,Mg@N-CDs can be summarized in the following Eqs. (1)–(7) [36,37].
(1) S2O8 2− + e- → SO4
·-
(2) Mg@N-CDs + e− → Mg@N-CDs ·-
(3) SO4
·− + TiO2 → TiO2
·+ + SO4 2-
(4) Mg@N-CDs ·− + TiO2
·+ → TiO2* + Mg@N-CDs
(5) TiO2* → TiO2 + hv

and/or
(6) Mg@N-CDs ·− + TiO2
·+ → TiO2 + Mg@N-CDs*
(7) Mg@N-CDs* → Mg@N-CDs + hv

ECL intensity-potential curves of immune-co-chemical sensor were shown in Fig. 4D. The AntiAFP in the probe specifically recognizes the targetAFP in the analytes. The presence of target result in an ECL "signal-off", and conversely, strong ECL signal corresponds to no target. What’s more, the ECL intensity restored obviously with the formation of AFP-Poly(DVB-Co-PBA) bioconjugate on AFP/AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE. On this basis, the accuracy and sensitivity for AFP detection could be improved extremely.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the compound Poly(DVB-Co-PBA) is presented in Fig. 5 A and C. It can be clearly seen from the image that Poly(DVB-Co-PBA) was a sphere with smooth surfaces and the particle size around 505 ± 21 nm. And inset image shows an enlarged SEM image of the Poly(DVB-Co-PBA). FT-IR of the prepared ECL-reporter Poly(DVB-Co-PBA) was recorded as shown in Fig. 5B. The attribution of each FT-IR peak is as follow: the wavenumber of 1342 cm−1 is the absorption peak of hydroxyl groupOH; 1608 cm-1 and 3018 cm−1 are the absorption peaks of CC and CH in benzene ring skeleton, respectively; 2237 cm−1 is the absorption peak of BO bond stretching vibration; and 1608 cm−1 is the absorption peak of conjugate CC bond vibration [15]. 1803 and 1922 cm−1 are the vibration of CH and CC in linear-RCHCH. These results demonstrated that microspheres were successfully synthesized. Moreover, the fluorescence spectra of Poly(DVB-Co-PBA) was measured. FL intensity achieved maximum of 4458 a.u. at 323.5 nm with excitation wavelength 258 nm (Fig. 5D). It further confirmed of that Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)-indicator has good fluorescence performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions of nanocomposites and test conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the excellent performance of Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE with highly ECL intensity, we explored optimal conditions for synthesis by measuring ECL intensity. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the ECL response showed an upward trend with the extending of ultrasonic-time raise from 10 to 30 min and then came a sharp decline. It was proofed [38] that small-sized Ti,Mg@N-CDs could boost the process of redox-reaction and accelerate the rate of electron-transfer, further enhancing the ECL intensity. And some structures were possible to be changed after a long ultrasonic treatment. Hence, 30 min was selected as the optimal time for ultrasonication. The ratio of Mg@N-CDs to NH2-TiO2 has a decisive effect on the intensity of prepared sensor. As shown in Fig. 6B, after coating of the nano-luminescent, the ECL signal sharply increased over the ratio of Mg@N-CDs to NH2-TiO2 between 2:1 and 1:1.5, and then slightly decreased. It may be explained that the reaction of SO4 ·− with excessive NH2-TiO2 hindered the generates of Mg@N-CDs* (excited state). On the contrary, less TiO2* is not conducive to the synergic enhancement of ECL intensity. Hence, 1:1.5 was chosen as the optimal Mg@N-CDs/NH2-TiO2 ratio.

It plays a great role to control the appropriate concentration of AntiAFP in the ECL assay. The Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE lose high ECL intensity after being incubated with AntiAFP. Fig. 6C showed the ECL value of Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE (AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE) over time extended by different AntiAFP-incubated sensor, indicating that the ECL signal of Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE gradually decreased with the increase of AntiAFP concentration. The ECL value of 10 μg mL−1 (6213 a.u.) was quite close to that of 70 ∼ 100 μg mL−1 (6230 a.u., 6199 a.u., 6233 a.u.), so the concentration of 50 μg mL-1 was chosen as the incubation concentration in the subsequent experiments to improve the sensitivity of detection and its economy. The electrochemical activity of the obtained AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE was reduced by 47.4 %. The ECL intensity was reduced by 50.8 % after the nonspecific sites of AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE were further blocked with 1% BSA, for part of electroactive sites of Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE were “turned off” with the inactive performance. The proposed ECL sensor ECL signal was affected by the incubation time and temperature. With the extending of incubation time, the ECL signal intensities showed apparent changes and reached the minimum (equilibrium) after incubation for 25 min in PBS at pH 7.4 (Fig. 6D). The ECL intensity reached a platform due to the limitation of the amount of AntiAFP. At the same time, it was seen in Fig. 6E that the ECL intensity showed a maximum quenching effect at the incubation temperature of 37 ℃. Hence, incubation time (25 min)/temperature (37 ℃) was chosen to be experimental conditions.

The pH value of the solution for Poly(DVB-Co-PBA) (300 μL 0.01 M NaOH) greatly affected the sensitivity of AFP detection. Fig. 6F showed the variations of ECL intensity with the solution pH. The optimal ECL response was achieved at pH 9.0 when the pH ranging from 6.5 to 11.0. This might be related with sp3-structure formed easier by the boric acid group of Poly(DVB-Co-PBA) under alkaline conditions, the sp3-structure specifically anchored the target of the AFP/AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE, in which five-membered cyclic borate esters were acted as electron donor to promote charge transfer, further, leading to the recovery of ECL intensity for sensing ultralow levels of AFP. Therefore, the optimum pH of the Poly(DVB-Co-PBA) solution was observed at 9.0.

An accurate ECL quantitative determination was performed under the optimal conditions to evaluate the accurate concentration of biomarker AFP. Fig. 7 A depicted the ECL intensity time curves, wherein the ECL intensity decreased gradually with the concentration of AFP increasing. A linear dependence between the relative change of the ECL intensity and the concentrations of AFP was obtained from 2.25 × 10−6 to 2250 ng mL-1(ΔF = F-F0) (Fig. 7B). As a result, a linear equation was expressed as ΔF = 317.0761 lgCAFP + 2219.4123, with the coefficient of determination R2 = 0.9925 in the figure. The detection limit was estimated to be 1.55 × 10-7 ng mL-1 basing on a signal-to-noise ratio of S/N = 3. More importantly, this ECL immunoassay presented a unique merit of dual recognition and realized both qualitative fast screening and quantitative ECL determination of AFP.

Furthermore, compared with other detection measures for AFP reported (Table 1 ), the fabricated immuno-co-chemical ECL-assay with a dual-recognition possessed a higher accuracy, sensitivity and wider linear range in this study.

The immune-co-chemical ECL-sensor for AFP was compared with that of other tumor markers sharing similar constituents with AFP and mixture with AFP (embryonic antigen (CEA), prostate-specific antigen (PSA), transferrin (TRF)) and some representative interfering substances (bovine serum albumin (BSA), glucose, immunoglobulins (lgG)) in the human serum to study the specificity, whose concentration was 10-folds of that of AFP. As shown in Fig. 8 A, the ECL-ΔF values of the immunosensor for CEA, PSA, TRF, BSA, glucose and lgG were much lower than that for AFP and the mixture. It suggested that these potential interfering biomolecules had negligible influence for the highly selective detection of AFP.

In addition, the eleven consecutive tests were analyzed to investigate the stability of developed sensor. From Fig. 8B, the performance of immune-co-chemical ECL-sensor was quite stable (with the RSD 3.25 %). Reproducibility was a non-negligible property for evaluating the performance of the prepared sensor. Different seven pretreated AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE were examined after incubating at 2.25 × 10−3 ng mL-1 AFP, and the signal-value (ΔF) from Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)-reporter were showed in Fig. 8C. The RSD of the intra-assay was 3.61 %, showing the sensor had a grateful repeating utilization. The developed immune-co-chemical ECL-sensor was also regenerated by immersing the Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)/AFP/AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE in 0.1 M NaOH solution for 2 min [6]. It resulted in the release of AFP from the electrode, thus leading to the recovery of the ECL intensity of AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE. Afterward, the refreshed immune-co-chemical ECL-sensor was used to detect AFP again. The whole cycle was repeated for 10 runs and the ECL responses dropped substantially after seven regeneration cycles (Fig. 8D), so an acceptable regeneration cycle should be seven (with the RSD 3.34 %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of AFP in human serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: A series concentration of standard AFP was added into the normal human serum diluted 10-folds with 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4) to demonstrate whether the proposed sensor could be used for real sample detection. The results of detection were showed in Table 2 with this method and ELISA, in which the recoveries were in the range of 96.04 % ∼ 111.22 % and 95.92%–108.00%, respectively. By comparison with the ELISA method, the RSDs obtained from the developed method showed acceptable feasibility, indicating the validity of the Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)-AFP/AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE for AFP detection in human serum for clinical diagnosis.

In summary, a simple, sensitive and accurate immune-co-chemical ECL-sensor was developed for AFP-assay via the novel luminophore Ti,Mg@N-CDs triggering and fluorescent microspheres Poly(DVB-Co-PBA) reporting. And it is comparable to sandwich ELISA and better than ELISA. The creational pattern of construction strategy (Poly(DVB-Co-PBA)-AFP/AntiAFP/Ti,Mg@N-CDs/GCE) showed a wider linear range from 2.25 10−6 to 2250 ng mL-1, ultra-low fg detection limit (LOD: 1.55 × 10-7 ng mL). The satisfactory analytical results in normal human serum samples also demonstrated the applicability of the proposed method in physiological sample matrix. In addition, based on the simple method of sonication-assisted chemistry at the presence of NH2-TiO2, nanocomposite Ti,Mg@N-CDs showed excellent long-term stability, good reproducibility and more favorable biocompatibility. Thus, this proposed method can be expanded readily for detecting other cancer biomarkers (such as prostate specific antigen) and has the potential for reliable diagnosis of cancer and other diseases with universality.

Jianping Guo: Visualization, investigation, data curation writing- Original draft preparation, revised manuscript. Shijie Li: Data curation, revised manuscript. Junying Wang: Conceptualization, methodology, reviewing and editing. Junping Wang: Conceptualization, methodology, reviewing and editing, supervision

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Innovation Project of Excellent Doctorial Dissertation of Tianjin University of Science and Technology (No. 2020010), The 68th China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M680870), “The National Key R&D Program of China” (No. 2016YFD0401202).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130548.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
The lists of instruments and reagents; Synthesis of Poly(DVB-Co-PBA).

Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Fang, J. Liu, J. Wang, H. Zhao, H. Ren, Z. Li, Dual signal amplification strategy of Au nanopaticles/ZnO nanorods hybridized reduced graphene nanosheet and multienzyme functionalized Au@ZnO composites for ultrasensitive electrochemical detection of tumor biomarker, Biosensors & bioelectronics, 97(2017) 218-25.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-sensitivity electrochemiluminescence probe with molybdenum carbides as nanocarriers for alpha-fetoprotein sensing
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhu, Q. Zhai, W. Gu, J. Li, E. Wang, High-Sensitivity Electrochemiluminescence Probe with Molybdenum Carbides as Nanocarriers for alpha-Fetoprotein Sensing, Analytical chemistry, 89(2017) 12108-14.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-free all-carbon nanohybrid for ultrasensitive photoelectrochemical immunosensing of alpha-fetoprotein
ACS Sens.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Zhou, H. Xue, Y. Zhang, Y. Lv, H. Li, S. Liu, et al., Metal-Free All-Carbon Nanohybrid for Ultrasensitive Photoelectrochemical Immunosensing of alpha-Fetoprotein, ACS sensors, 3(2018) 1385-91.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The photoelectrochemical exploration of multifunctional TiO2 mesocrystals and its enzyme-assisted biosensing application
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Dai, S. Zhang, L. Gong, Y. Li, G. Xu, Y. Lin, et al., The photoelectrochemical exploration of multifunctional TiO2 mesocrystals and its enzyme-assisted biosensing application, Biosensors & bioelectronics, 72(2015) 18-24.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A graphene oxide-based label-free electrochemical aptasensor for the detection of alpha-fetoprotein
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Yang, F. Zhang, Z. Wang, Q. Liang, A graphene oxide-based label-free electrochemical aptasensor for the detection of alpha-fetoprotein, Biosensors & bioelectronics, 112(2018) 186-92.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of aptamer responsive DNA functionalized hydrogels for the sensitive detection of alpha-fetoprotein using SERS method
Bioconjug. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Wang, Y. Hu, N. Jiang, J. Wang, M. Yu, X. Zhuang, Preparation of Aptamer Responsive DNA Functionalized Hydrogels for the Sensitive Detection of alpha-Fetoprotein Using SERS Method, Bioconjugate chemistry, 31(2020) 813-20.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of novel electrochemical immunosensor by mussel-inspired chemistry and surface-initiated PET-ATRP for the simultaneous detection of CEA and AFP
React. Funct. Polym.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Wang, X. Zhao, H. Chen, L. Bai, H. Xu, W. Wang, et al., Fabrication of novel electrochemical immunosensor by mussel-inspired chemistry and surface-initiated PET-ATRP for the simultaneous detection of CEA and AFP, Reactive and Functional Polymers, 154(2020) 104632.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of AFP with an ultra-sensitive giant magnetoimpedance biosensor
Sens. Actuators B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhu, Q. Zhang, X. Li, H. Pan, J. Wang, Z. Zhao, Detection of AFP with an ultra-sensitive giant magnetoimpedance biosensor, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 293(2019) 53-8.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jianping Guo is currently a doctor course student in Department of Food Science, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her researches mainly focus on the development and application of electrochemiluminescence sensors.

Shijie Li was received his Ph.D. degree from Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Currently, she is a postdoctoral researcher at Nankai University, China. Her researches mainly focus on the immunoassay towards hazardous substances in food.

Junying Wang received his Ph.D. in 2004 from Beijing Forestry University, now he is serving as a professor in The Biotechnology Research Institute (BRI) of Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences (CAAS). His researches mainly focus on protein and genetic engineering.

Junping Wang received his Ph.D. in 2003 from China Agricultural University, now he is serving as a professor in Tianjin University of Science and Technology. His researches mainly focus on Food Safety and Food Science.
